tokens: Put it on top of the ETB.
Okay, take out your purge valve.
transcript.speaker: Shepard

tokens: Why don't you turn the (PLSS) fan off here?
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: We'd like to confirm that you have closed the O2 valve on your PLSS.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: I'll double-check it, Bruce, but...(Pause) I verify it's closed.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: Okay, go to Suit Flow on the
transcript.speaker: Shepard

tokens: Why don't you help me with my water.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: You'll have to help me with the water connection.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: I said, I can't close that one.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: (Long Pause) How about popping this one in for me too, will you?
transcript.speaker: Shepard

tokens: What kind of fitting was in there?
transcript.speaker: Shepard

tokens: You'd stuck this one in that one.
I didn't do it very well.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: In going through the comm checklist, we'd like to leave the S-band Transmitter and Receiver in Secondary.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: We'd like you to pull the circuit breaker on the TV camera at this time.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: We're about ready to give you some weights on return equipment.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: We're ready to come up with the first bags.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: Houston, let me tell you what we've done.
Remember (that) Al said that we brought in the small rocks from the comprehensive sample area in one weigh bag.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: We couldn't get them in the SRC.
We got the contingency sample here.
And it so happens that the material cracked on the contingency sample bag, and it's leaking.
So we're putting it in the weigh bag with these other rocks.
And the weight of that total combination is 5 pounds.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: The contingency sample's in that weigh bag with a total weight of 5 pounds.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: (Long Pause) And, Houston, the next bag has two toy-sized football rocks in it.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: And that's going into the left-hand storage compartment.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: Left-hand storage compartment with two little-league footballs; 15 pounds.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Both of those rock bags are going to left-hand storage compartment.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: That's the one with the contingency sample and the comprehensive and the football ones, right?
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Ed, if you'll stand by just 1 minute, I need to copy a read...
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: ...down from Stu, and I'll be right back to you.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: I'm ready to listen to you for a while.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: We want you to be discriminating about our samples now.
We have the comprehensive rocks in the left-hand storage compartment.
The comprehensive fines, however, are in the SRC.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: The comprehensive rocks in the storage compartment and the fines in the SRC.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: (Long Pause) And Mag MM is replacing (mag) II.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: We replaced magazine JJ on the Commander's camera with LL and II on the LMP's camera with MM.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: We've got L-L and M-M on there now.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Verify which are the two 16-millimeter mags that have been used?
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: Did you understand my question, Gordon?
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: I didn't realize it was a question.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: We have three 16-millimeter mags, of which we only used two.
Can you tell me which two we used?
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: I'll give you an answer in a minute here.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Our records show that the used magazines are Charlie-Charlie and Echo-Echo.
And Delta-Delta should be the unused one.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: We're making a slight deviation to our storage plan in the ETB.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: In addition to the three 16-millimeter mags called for (in the checklist), we're also taking back out the one we didn't get used today.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: And in addition to the black-and-white (70mm) Mag K-K, we're taking back out Juliett-Juliett.
We've only used 40 frames off of that, and we've got very few pictures of the LM and other appropriate-type shots.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: Juliett-Juliett, you're also going to take out on the second EVA.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Just following you in the checklist here and looking ahead, it looks to us like you'll probably get to the EVA debriefing in an hour and a half, about 122 (hours).
Does this agree with your estimate?
We just wanted to know to be sure to have the right people standing by.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Well, it's not on this card.
How far is it on the Lunar Surface Checklist?
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: And we find that weighs 43 (terrestrial) pounds.
transcript.speaker: Shepard

tokens: And if you're just proceeding down the card with no changes to it, well, then we'll figure it out when you get to the debriefing.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Listen, we've had enough thrills today without changing our checklist.
transcript.speaker: Shepard

tokens: We didn't really mean to suggest that, Al, we just...Disregard.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: I'm standing by for my T3s.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: I'm standing by for our lift-off table.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: I've got them for (Command Module orbits number) 20 through 25, if you're ready.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: It's a little late for 20 (because Roosa has already gone overhead on Rev 20).
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: I'll give it to you anyway.
To fill in all the blanks.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Short status on the CSM up there: He's in good shape.
Done everything on the timeline right on the money.
The only problem on board seems to be the Topo camera.
The plane change went well with a Delta-V of 671 feet per second, about 8 or 9 feet per second more than pre-flight, which was just to circularize the orbit slightly.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Is he using the 500...Let's see, what is it?
The 500 millimeter in place of the Hycon?
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: Actually the substitution won't occur until tomorrow, and that's most likely what we'll do.
We're running one more test on the Hycon to verify that it is indeed hopeless, and then we'll back it up with the 500.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: We've finished our meal, whatever meal it was, and we're off and running on the PLSS feedwater collection.
transcript.speaker: Shepard

tokens: Please be advised that one RCU weighs 0.38 kilograms.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: Houston, the Commander's feedwater is 0.25 kilograms.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: Houston, the LMP's feedwater, one point...No, wait a minute - point one nine kilograms.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: Okay, we've arrived at the EVA debriefing block.
But, in the meantime, let us say to the medics that we haven't had any medication, that the Commander's
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: I have 10 questions having to do with the EVA.
We don't want elaborate answers, because they, of course, cut into your sleep period.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: A couple of general comments first, though.
The CDR's EKG electrode is erratic and (correcting himself) the data from it is erratic.
We were going to ask you to do what you can by way of applying external pressure or any other good ideas you might have there to maybe get it working again; but do not unzip your suit to get to it.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: We'll try a little pressure in the right places.
In the meantime, we also would like to report that we have completed both O2 top-off and the water recharge for both PLSSs.
And the condition of the crew is excellent.
transcript.speaker: Shepard

tokens: About the start of the next EVA: we plan not to wake you up any earlier then scheduled, but if you are awake and ready to go, we'll be ready to support for an early egress on the next one.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: We'd like to plan on an early egress anyway, I think, so that we'll be in a position to get the full EVA-2 and still get back in at the regularly scheduled timeline.
transcript.speaker: Shepard

tokens: The LM status is completely ops (operations) normal.
The consumables are in good shape.
We believe that the steerable antenna problem you had during descent was probably due to a multipath reception at AOS, and we're predicting, now, that it will probably work okay for ascent.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Question number 1 about EVA-1: How do you feel about your planned second EVA - now that you've done the first - especially in terms of time and terrain?
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Well, I think that the second EVA will go a little more smoothly with respect to the timeline.
It's not as complicated as far as the equipment is concerned.
We don't spend as much time moving around with scientific equipment.
It's primarily a geological traverse once the thing has gotten by the first few minutes.
And we should be able to be on the timeline and hang onto that real well.
And we, of course, are again counting on at least a 30-minute extension to the nominal time, so that's the reason we'd like to start early.
transcript.speaker: Shepard

tokens: Do you feel the terrain will be any problem?
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: We had no difficulty at all in traversing the terrain.
As a matter of fact, we were bounding along, even with the (ALSEP) barbell and the MET.
The traversing is extremely easy, although we have a rolling landscape and lots of craters to circumnavigate.
I believe from looking at Cone, we'll be able to get up there with no trouble at all.
transcript.speaker: Shepard

tokens: The undulating terrain is just a surprise.
It's not that much more difficult.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: Second question is: would you please describe the rim of Doublet, especially the blockiness?
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Well, I don't think you'd call Doublet a blocky rim.
The craters - North and South Doublet - of course, are both older craters and have subdued rims.
There are some blocks of ejecta at and near the vicinity of the rims, and there are a few blocks down inside.
For example, we can look at the west wall of south Doublet from here and see a few fairly good-sized rocks, perhaps 3 or 4 feet at the largest.
But I really wouldn't call it a blocky rim.
It's a fairly well subdued rim.
transcript.speaker: Shepard

tokens: The biggest blocks we could see on the rim of Doublet correspond to these large ones (north of the LM) I pointed out in my TV pan.
There are some of that size or maybe a little larger, but the population is miniscule compared with the total rim area of Doublet.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: Third question: how deep is the DPS (Descent Propulsion System, pronounced "dips") erosion crater?
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: It's not very deep at all.
The photographs will show that it's, perhaps, only 4 inches at maximum depth.
transcript.speaker: Shepard

tokens: Okay, and can you describe the lineations and how far out they went, their orientation, and direction.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Are these the lineations that I referred to earlier, or are you talking about lineations from the DPS engine?
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: The ones that you referred to earlier, Ed.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Okay, they're there; and I saw evidences of them in directions different than the exhaust would cause, but there just simply was not time to look at them.
We'll have to look at them tomorrow.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: Next question: on the football samples, were they documented?
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: They were documented with a stereopair "before", in the case of both samples.
And they were taken from the crater which is located at...Let's see, (to himself, consulting the
transcript.speaker: Shepard

tokens: Next question: did you notice any variations in soil-mechanics characteristics at various locations where legs or poles were pushed in, such as the solar-wind staff, the flagstaff, geophone anchors, penetrometer, and so forth?
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Yeah, there are a few places around - primarily fill or rather the throwouts from craters, or what are obviously near the rims of craters - (that) have a softer material around them than there is just in general.
However, there are so many craters that you find the soft material quite often, but generally on the fresher ones.
Along my traverse - rather along the thumper geophone line - there are two or three fairly fresh craters along that line that had some quite soft material around them.
And it was a matter of sinking in 2, 3 or 4 inches instead of a normal half to three-quarters (of an inch) that we're sinking in out here.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: On the surface features of rocks, marks...Well, we'd like a description of the surface features of the rocks.
If they are marked, variations in rounding, angularity, grain size, size distribution, shape, texture, and color.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: You're getting into stuff that we're going to have to look at tomorrow.
We just barely had time to finish the ALSEP and get back.
The rocks I see from the cockpit, there are some rounded rocks; I can see two or three that are varied, that have some rounding on top.
As far as granularity, crystal size, et cetera, et cetera, we didn't have time to look at any of that.
We'll have to wait until tomorrow.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: Okay, Ed, this next question probably falls in the same category.

I'll read it in case you have anything to say about it, and that is to describe the regolith, the general nature, fragment distribution, fragment shapes, variations in texture, color, surface patterns, and firmness.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Okay, we can give a quick one on that.
I think we've already done most of it.
The regolith is mostly a mouse brown - or sometimes looking gray - a powdery material.
Almost like a chalk, ground up; it's that thin and that fine-grained.

There are a few rocks scattered around; the population (is) less than a percent (that is, less than one percent of the surface is covered with rocks), ranging in size from 5 to 6 - well, I guess, 2 or 3 centimeters - but the ones that are obvious, that aren't buried, are 5 or 6 centimeters; up to the largest ones that I've seen are the ones I showed you in the (TV) pan, which are 3, 4, or 5 feet across.

The distribution is less than 1 percent, but you see a few of these blocks sitting all around the landscape as far as you can see, and I guess they're even out over toward Doublet, which we didn't say was blocky, but these smaller ones might not be visible at that distance.
And I can look to the north, and I don't see too many on the far edge of the crater over there either; but it could be that that's too far away to be able to see them well.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: Okay, did you notice any variations in color or surface patterns or texture?
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: To me, it looked all about the same, as far as the general regolith here is concerned; but, again, we haven't looked at it that carefully - or I didn't look at it that carefully - just because of the press of time.
By and large it is all this very fine-grain material with a few scattered rocks on top of it.
Let's see if we can do a better job of describing it tomorrow for you.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: Yeah, I think that's generally true.

We can see areas, for example, looking normally out the window - that is in the cross-Sun direction, in my case to the south - where the rocks in one ejecta pattern of fairly large rocks of 3 or 4 feet appear to have a very lighter gray texture to them in comparison to the gray-brown which Ed just described, which would be the regolith.
And I notice that this crater that sits out here to the 9:30 position of the LM is also a brighter crater.
It's a newer crater; it has a raised rim.
And it has a different color, for example, than does the crater directly behind it about the same distance, which is much older and a darker gray.
transcript.speaker: Shepard

tokens: I think, generally, we'll find some variations in texture throughout tomorrow's traverse.
transcript.speaker: Shepard

tokens: The only place I thought I saw glass - and I didn't have time to confirm it - was in a very small crater along the thumper line.
It looked like there was a little pool of glass at the bottom; and this crater was only about 2 foot across and maybe 8 inches deep.

It had quite a bit of small chunky material in it, but it had a different color and looked very glassy at the bottom; and I didn't have time to go back and look at it, but I'm sure there's some more of that around.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: Last question is, how abundant were fillets?
Do those by the LM appear to be disturbed by the DPS (Descent Propulsion System)?
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: I think we find some fillets.
I don't know whether the percentage is as high as 50 percent or not of the surface rocks.
But, yes, there is some filleting, and you'll notice in the small football-size rocks, there was a fillet pattern around them.
There is filleting here, of course, very close to the LM, and it's hard to tell whether it's natural or whether it's from the LM exhaust.
transcript.speaker: Shepard

tokens: Roger; that's all the pre-prepared questions.
I'll check and make sure there's no last minute ones here.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Okay, I might comment that looking at our footprints - with the MET track and our footprints out to the ALSEP site (and) over to the (TV) camera - both looking down-Sun and cross-Sun, that the fresh dirt we've kicked up and turned over is noticeably darker (and) browner than the more mousy-brown, lighter-brown undisturbed regolith that's on top.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: (Long Pause) Ed, the last time you left the ALSEP site, can you give us the...The last time you happened to look at the number 1 geophone, was it still in place properly?
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: All 3 geophones were in good shape when I left them.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: We're having some problem with the signal strength on the ALSEP.
Is there any chance that the Central Station could have been disturbed such that the antenna alignment would no longer be proper?
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: No, I don't think either one of us went by there again.
I took some pictures of it, but nobody touched it after the antenna was aligned.
transcript.speaker: Shepard

tokens: Well, I touched it, of course, in turning number 5 switch off and on; but, gee, that didn't do anything.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: Okay, Houston, we'd like to proceed with the checklist now.

I'd like to make a request that we plan starting the timeline tomorrow 1 hour early, so that we'll be able to get in a 30-minute extension and still have time after we get back in to have a leisurely re-stowage.
transcript.speaker: Shepard

tokens: We'll go along with that all the way.
We'll plan to start 1 hour early; in fact, if...We're not recommending it, but if you want to start earlier than that, we'll be ready to support.
You don't need to worry about support here.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Our nominal rest period ends at 130 hours and 30 minutes (129:50 transcript time).
We'll be a good 6 hours and a half (into the rest period by then).
Why don't we say 129 hours even.
(128:20 transcript time) You wake us up if we haven't called you by then.
transcript.speaker: Shepard

tokens: Okay, we're checking that figure, and I'll give you a confirmation here if I get it.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: I don't think we're going to sleep more than 6 hours anyway.
And we'll be in bed so that we have 6 and a half hours.
The way we're going now, all we have to do is rig the
transcript.speaker: Shepard

tokens: Houston, if you concur, I'll go ahead (as per
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: We noticed your H2O separator in the PLSSs were running kind of fast.
Do you have any problem at all with water in your suits?
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: None that we've recognized so far.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: We'll go along with...We'll ring the alarm at 129 hours, and I think that completes all the items we have for you.
You are clear to go ahead with the last three steps before configuring for sleep.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: We'll press on with that now.
transcript.speaker: Shepard

tokens: Don't bother to acknowledge, but we're getting ready to do a station handover.
You may hear a burst of noise.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton
